what_it_does: This file is a posted noticeboard note (a paragraph comment by Tim) that records his personal preference for voice input and his experience using iPhone speech-to-text for annotations and comments.
observations: Tim prefers voice input, especially on his phone
All annotations and comments were created with iPhone built-in speech-to-text, not typed
Speech-to-text is inaccurate enough that he constantly corrects and types
He has silent pauses in the middle of sentences while thinking
He currently speaks punctuation aloud and is unsure whether automating it would be better
He states that everything being worked on is in service of him exclusively
standouts: Explicit framing that all work is 'in service of me exclusively'
A raw, unedited speech-to-text transcript is preserved as the primary content
Strong voice-first user preference is surfaced directly by the sole user
conventions: Follows the noticeboard item YAML frontmatter schema with id, address, type, author_session, channel, links, history, etc.
Uses a commented_on link to relate to another board item
Breaks polished-document conventions by preserving speech-to-text artifacts such as repetition, mid-sentence restarts, and spoken punctuation
concerns: The content is a stream-of-consciousness transcript with no explicit actionable requirement, which may make design extraction ambiguous
notes: Tim's raw voice-input note explaining his preference for speech-to-text and declaring that the system is being built solely for him.
questions: Should the annotation interface explicitly support iPhone dictation quirks like spoken punctuation and mid-sentence pauses?
Does 'everything being worked on is in service of me exclusively' define a single-user product scope or just a prioritization principle?
Which specific speech-to-text errors are common enough that the workflow should mitigate them?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated as a paragraph comment and it is exactly that — a first-person, voice-dictated note about input preferences.
intention_signals: To surface the author's real-world input modality and its constraints so that annotation and review workflows can be designed around voice-driven use.
novelty: It preserves an unusually candid, unedited dictation transcript and explicitly frames the entire project as serving one person.
contribution: Provides a first-person account of voice-input behavior and constraints that a fused system should keep visible when designing annotation interfaces and input modalities.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard note by Tim describing his exclusive use of iPhone speech-to-text for annotations, his preference for voice input, and the personal scope of the tooling.
intention_for_embedding: To communicate Tim's voice-first usage patterns and constraints to inform the design of annotation and review workflows.
